FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Data Release 5.0, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10 years (Methods: "Data were obtained from the ABCD Study® (Data Release 5.0.), an ongoing, multi-site longitudinal study with adolescents recruited from 21 sites across the United States").

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using structural equation modeling (SEM) in Mplus 7.4, prospectively associating sleep and sensation-seeking at Year 2 with mature screen usage at Year 3 in n=3687 ABCD participants.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article published in Sleep (2025); no indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable (time on phone, time on SM, named platform, texting, video chat, app use, MPIQ, SMAQ, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own numeric coefficient anywhere in the paper. The outcomes are mature-rated video game use and R-rated movie watching. Step 2 not reached. Per binding clarification #4, mature-rated/R-rated content alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable; content rating is not modality. The IVs are sleep measures (objective Fitbit) and sensation-seeking (personality scale). No qualifying smartphone/SM variable is present in any analysis.

notes: Excluded via FT-EC2. Paper examines sleep and sensation-seeking as predictors of mature-rated video gaming and R-rated movie watching. Neither the IVs nor DVs constitute a qualifying phone/SM variable. Mature/R-rated content criterion is the binding clarification that sinks this paper at IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Release 5.1 data; 495 Android users from the Year-4 follow-up subsample ("Individuals participated in the ABCD study. Release 5.1 data included 4754 participants at Year-4 follow-up").

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis comparing EARS-passively-sensed smartphone app usage with self-reported screen time, and examining associations with behavioral problems and problematic technology use in 495 ABCD participants.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in npj Mental Health Research (2025); no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: EARS-logged social media use (passively sensed smartphone app time, a phone/SM-specific variable) is paired with externalizing symptoms (individual-trait behavioral outcome) with β=0.07 (95% CI: 0.03-0.12, p=0.002) and internalizing symptoms β=0.05 (95% CI: 0.01-0.09, p=0.028 for self-reported social media; Fig. 3 / Results section). Self-reported social media use is also paired with externalizing (β=0.06, 95% CI: 0.01-0.11) and internalizing symptoms. These are individual-trait-level outcomes (behavioral/mental health). The other side qualifies.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple β coefficients and 95% CIs reported for the SM ↔ behavioral outcomes relationships (Fig. 3, Results section); OR estimates for logistic regressions also reported (Table 3a/b).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses performed (linear mixed models with family as random effect, logistic regression); numeric results reported throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: The primary design examining SM ↔ behavioral outcomes is cross-sectional (associations measured at Year-4 follow-up without SM predicting a future wave of outcomes). The cross-sectional carve-out applies: SM is DV in cross-sectional analyses (e.g., EARS SM use regressed on demographics; SM use correlated with CBCL outcomes cross-sectionally). EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper qualifies via EARS-logged social media use (phone-passively-sensed) paired with externalizing/internalizing symptoms. The measurement discrepancy focus is the paper's framing but it reports genuine SM ↔ individual-trait associations. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Longitudinal data from the U.S. ABCD Study (4.0 release), baseline 2016-2018, 21 recruitment sites, ages 10-14 years, N=10,757 ("We utilised longitudinal data from baseline to Year 2 of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (4.0 release)").

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis using mixed-effects models to examine associations between baseline parental monitoring/family conflict and screen time outcomes at Year 1 and Year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in Acta Paediatrica (2024); no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Social media use (hours/day, a phone/SM-specific modality per the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire) has its own separate B coefficient in Table 2 (Model 2, Family conflict row: B=0.008 [95% CI -0.003, 0.02]; Model 2, Parental monitoring row: B=0.007 [95% CI -0.04, 0.05]). The predictor variables are parental monitoring and family conflict, which qualify as individual-trait-level variables (parenting practices and family functioning are listed as qualifying individual-trait constructs). Although the social media coefficients are not statistically significant, numeric estimates are present. Additionally, texting (B coefficients in Table 2) and video chat also have separate estimates paired with the same individual-trait predictors.

FT_IC3.evidence: B coefficients and 95% CIs reported for all screen modalities in Table 2 (adjusted models); inferential statistics throughout.

FT_IC4.evidence: Mixed-effects models with robust standard errors; numeric results reported for all modalities and predictors in Table 2.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions hold: (a) longitudinal design (baseline predictors, outcomes at Year 1 and Year 2 follow-up); (b) screen time including social media is the DV in every analysis; (c) no analysis anywhere in the paper uses social media or screen time as IV. Predictors are parental monitoring and family conflict (not SM). No cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV is reported with its own estimates (baseline screen time appears only as a covariate in the longitudinal models, not as a DV in a separately reported cross-sectional model). EC7 applies.
notes: Paper is excluded under FT-EC7. Longitudinal design with SM/screen time as DV throughout; parental monitoring and family conflict are the predictors. No SM-as-IV analysis exists anywhere. No separate cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis reported (baseline screen time is used only as a covariate adjustment variable).
